title: 2020 Fire Program Comprehensive Program Review (Part 1)
text:
College of the Siskiyous
Fire Technology Program
Instructional Program Review
2020-21
Michael Wilson

-- 1 of 28 --

1. Program Description:
The Fire Technology curriculum prepares students for careers in a variety of areas in the Fire
Service. These areas are Specialized, Paid, Volunteer, and Degree applicable. All of these programs
are designed to meet all Local, State, and Federal levels. These programs consist of Fire Core
classes (6), State Firefighter I Academy (Spring and Fall), CalFire Basic Academy (Summer),
Firefighter II, Rescue Track, HazMat Track, Company Officer Track, Instructor Track, Wild-Land
Firefighter, and Command Track.
The programs at College of the Siskiyous allows students to complete and Associate of Arts degree
or an Associate in Science degree for transfer to a California State University. The focus of this
program allows the student to be educated as much as possible in order to be competitive in the
Fire Service of today.
a) Current Program Organizational Structure:
The Fire Technology Program is comprised of the following staff positions:
1. Mike Wilson, Chief of Fire Technology, Faculty (District funded) – reports to Mark Klever, Dean
of Career and Technical Education (CTE)
2. Numerous Adjunct Instructors and Instructional aides to support the Academies and Stand-
alone programs. They all report to Chief Wilson.
b) Primary Purpose:
The purpose of the Fire Technology Program is to provide educational opportunities to our
Students so they are successful in their individual goals.
c) Program Demographics:
According to the data provided, between Fall 2014 and Spring 2019, the Fire Technology
Program had 2,002 students. The students are broken down into the following demographics:

d) Services:
The Fire Technology Program offers a wide range of classes to serve the High School and adult level
students that are seeking certificates, degrees, career opportunities, and professional
development. This program meets or exceeds the requirements for hire for Local government
departments volunteer and or paid, State (CalFire), and the Federal (USFS). It also provides the
students with expanded skills to meet the Urban Search and Rescue Tasks Force requirements. The
Academies meet the State Fire Training requirements as they are currently listed.
e) Delivery:
The Fire Technology Programs are delivered in a variety of options,from the 18 week State Fire
Training Academy, the specialized programs (stand-alone) Rescue Systems I, Confined Space,
Trench Rescue, Auto extrication, Low Angle Rope Rescue, Tower operations, Wild-land operations,
Safety and Survival, Rapid Intervention Crew, Company Officer, Instructor I and II, Swift Water
Technician, Volunteer Fire Fighter Certification, Hazard Materials, Avalanche I and II, CalFire Basic
training, Incident Command, Driver operator, and additionally the Basic Core classes ( Fire
Protection Organization, Fire Prevention, Fire Protection Systems, Building Construction, Fire
Behavior and Combustion, Fire and Emergency Services Safety and Survival), and also an
opportunity for work experience with an local/state department.
f) Updated Curriculum:
The curriculum based on its program needs are updated per a cycle determined by the State Fire
Training or the College System determinates. Due to the constant changes within the field of
operations, curriculum may be updated more frequently depending on State mandates. Overall,
these courses prepare students for direct employment in their field of study.
g) Course Information:
The courses that we offered are based on the Trade Department needs. We provide at a minimum
of one (1) State Fire Academy, and three of the six (6) core classes per semester. The addition
classes are determined by the needs of the department training needs or recertification requests.
2. External Factors:

-- 15 of 28 --

title: 2020 Fire Program Comprehensive Program Review (Part 4)
text:
a) Budgetary constraints including personnel, support, props, and the improvement of
existing buildings and needs for a new training tower and training station.
b) Nearest Fire program is within 70 miles to the South of us and their expansion could affect
our enrollment and future growth.
c) Job Market is limited in Northern California and the seasonal employment may lead to the
exiting of students from the area seeking more full time employment and career opportunities.
d) Marketing our students is a high priority and our Academy and programs do a very good
job preparing them for this challenge. Continuing to find ways to provide a one stop best education
format needs to continue to be at the for-front of these Factors.
3. Progress on Outcomes Assessment:
a) SLO’s evaluated each semester and evaluated for alignment with programmatic SLO’s
b) The programs have been updated to meet State Fire requirements, equipment has been
updated to meet those standards and future needs are being addressed
c) Continual program and course assessment throughout the next four years
4. Institutional Program Effectiveness Indicators:
The institutional program effectiveness was touched upon in the program review. Full-time Faculty
of one with part-time adjunct instructors currently working in the Fire Profession is currently
working to meet the students’ needs to succeed.

5. Other Unit-Specific Quantitative and Qualitative Research:
A) Certification Information are based on the program in which they attended. The Academy
success rate is has continued to have a 3%-5% drop rate with the factors being time commitment
being greater than the student considered is. Test scores not meeting the requirements of the
syllabus. Stand-alone classes have a greater success rate mainly due to the 1 to 5 day program and
the ratio of instructors to students. The Core Class attendances range is continually increasing in
size and for the same reason of the Academy, the drop rate is consistent with time commitments
and test scores related to the attendance model.
b) Students are receiving State certifications however, being able to tack these are very
limited due to the requirements of time in the profession and the documentation provided by their
local department to the State.
c) We are doing our best to track these students with the request of when they are hired to
send the program manager that information as we can monitor of successes.
6. Evaluation:
a) Women are underrepresented in this program however; we are seeing a small rise
currently.
b) Currently we do not have the staff to provide online classes in this area.
c) Our Partnerships with the Trade Departments are very strong.
d) Best practices are updated and implemented as technology and policies dictate.
e) Our operation needs to grow to stay current in the job market, with that the facilities need
to grow as well.
f) Resources are limited due to the need to increase the facilities.

-- 21 of 28 --

title: 2020 Fire Program Comprehensive Program Review (Part 7)
text:
g) Full- time staffing needs to increase as well as a fulltime-classified position to enhance the
program to its potential.
h) We have a very active advisory made up of a wide diverse range of trade departments,
staff, councilors, deans, and budgetary personnel.
i) Faculty flex opportunities have met some of these items, yet some trainings in our field
affect the weekend and credit for two days is only maintained as one. The workload of one staff
member does not allow these areas of training to be achieved easily without the financial impact of
coverage.
j) The Fire Technology program and stand-alone classes me or exceed the State Fire Training
requirements.
7. Vision:
The future of the Fire Technology program is dependent on the ability to stay above the job market
interest. Making the students as marketable as possible with up to date trainings and state of the
art props to maintain those high standards. Our current tower is useable only because of the
contract with the local department in ownerships and therefore a rental contract had to be
developed for use because it does not meet the current standards from the college system. In
order for this to come more in line with this process a new approved tower has been approved and
moving forward as requested. The need for a training fire station would serve several purposes by
housing our rolling stock from the weather, provide the station atmosphere that the students need
to understand, provide additional classroom and housing areas, and due to the change in weather,
conditions of our area interior props can be developed to reduce the postponement of classes.
Increase in full-time staffing as the program was designed would allow one position to coordinate
the program while the core class instructor would carry the teaching load of the core classes and
be able to be used in the instructor roll to add to and fill in for adjunct instructors that for whatever
reason was unable to show up. The addition of a full-time Instructional aid (classified) position
would enhance the ability to maintain the equipment and aid to the instructors whenever their
classes are schedule throughout the year. This would not be a replacement for the split position
currently used to aid the Academy when it is in session. Changing the forest plan to allow the
expansion of the Fire Technology program as a whole would allow us to add additional classes that
are not currently offered in the area. Although we are making great strides in replacement of
equipment currently used for our program, we would need to continue to develop an exchange
program to meet current standards as those item meet their lifetime schedule.

-- 22 of 28 --

title: 2020 Fire Program Comprehensive Program Review (Part 8)
text:
8. Progress on Prior Goals:
This is the first Program Review that I have been involved in, so the term Progress on Prior Goals
may not have the same information as prior Coordinators. I would say that we continue to look at
ways to increase enrollment through offering classes and programs that meet the student’s needs
and the trade departments that have hiring standards. We have been replacing our used
equipment that have met their life timelines. We will continue to monitor the progress of our
current programs and adjust to those needs as they arise.
9. Four-Year Action Plan and Continuous Quality Improvement Proposal (CQIP)
Non-Academic Program Review
Continuous Quality Improvement Proposal (CQIP)
Personnel request
Issue:
Increasing the staffing levels of the Fire Technology Program, Replacement/Additional Approved Tower,
Additional Training Fire Station
Vision:
The College vision has been to provide increased staffing where the justifications of personnel are needed
to enhance the programs offered. This is in line with the Institutional Master Plan. The
replacement/Additional Approved Tower is needed to reduce the financial rental contract of the “City
Tower” and to add an approved tower that meets the current College standards of the non con-x style
towers. The addition of a Fire Training Station serves several purposes which include storage of rolling
stock of all three CTE programs (Fire, EMS, Law) from the weather conditions. It provides additional
classroom over flow options, addresses the lack of sleeping areas not available from the dorm setting. This
all adds to the real life, hands on life style of the Firefighter profession. Increasing replacement options and
expansion of the equipment and grounds to meet the increase demand of training to insure the marketable
ability of students at College of the Siskiyous.
Background:
The Fire Technology Program is a three stage program, made up from the Academies, Core, and Specialty
classes. The past history of these programs was covered under the Chain of Command that flowed from the
Dean, Coordinator of the Academy, Core Instructor, Adjunct Instructors to support the Academies, and the
Instruction Aids to support the Adjunct Instructors.
Rationale:

-- 23 of 28 --

title: 2020 Fire Program Comprehensive Program Review (Part 9)
text:
Due to the increase in enrollment from 7 to 33 in the Core Classes and the continuation of the Firefighter I
and the expansion of the Firefighter II programs, and the increase in the amount of specialty classes and
their increased enrollment numbers it has become apparent that as we move into the future of our
programs more staffing will have to be considered. The programs are growing and therefore the leadership
of those programs needs to grow with them as well. Under the current staffing level the need to reach out
to future students is not to the level it could be which has the potential to increase the student enrollment
numbers to help offset the impact of the cost. Replacement of the current “City Tower” brings us in line
with approved props and reduces the rental contact with the local department. The Training station would
continue to provide the ability to house overflow students, secure costly equipment, protection for the
weather to all three CTE programs, add to the classroom shortage issue, provide the ability for weather
controlled training, and provide a true to life response model of the firefighter profession. Replacement of
the current flashover prop that the cost of repairs and live span are reaching. Replacement of equipment
that has met its life span with equal to replacement. Expand the forestry plan to allow the expansion of the
training grounds to meet the increase training demands of the fire profession.
Resource Allocation:
Currently, I don’t see how reallocation could be used in this situation. The need for educated professionals
to the standards that are set before us are not available from another department of the College to be
reassigned.
Recommendation:
Currently the program has one fulltime staff member that Coordinates all of the programs, Teaches the
Core Classes, Teaches at the Academy level, Orders and maintains the levels of equipment and directs the
repair of the equipment and props. Is the Liaison officer between the College and the “Feeder” Fire
Departments. The Coordinator is also responsible for all logistical needs of all classes that make up the total
program.
A:
The recommendation to increase one (1) classified position assigned to all equipment and prop needs,
maintaining all inventory and testing dates. Prepares work orders under the direction of the Coordinator or
his/her designee.
B:
The recommendation to increase one (1) Fulltime staff to share the teaching loads of the program, help
coordinate the outside and special classes in conjunction with the current Coordinator. This would also
allow the program to branch out to meet the needs of students looking for and promoting the College of
the Siskiyous as a total package.
Both positions would follow the standard wage and hiring process as set by the Administration and hiring
committee.
C:

-- 24 of 28 --

title: 2020 Fire Program Comprehensive Program Review (Part 10)
text:

Replace the current Tower with an approved tower that continues to meet the State Fire Training and
College requirements of the non- con x style. (In process, expected to be completed Fall 2021)
D:
Addition of a Training Fire Station to meet the growing needs of security, classroom shortage, training and
housing issues. USDA currently provides a zero percent loan that would cover the project.
E:
Continue to invest in the replacement of equipment that has met it scheduled life usage cycle.
F:
Work within the forestry plan for expansion of props and areas to meet the ever-increasing demands of the
Fire Profession education model.
